questions.question: According to this course, what does the knowledge and awareness e-learning NOT qualify a care worker to do?
options: Assess, diagnose or treat any condition of the mouth or teeth
Follow the person's mouth care plan
Notice and report a mouth problem
Support a person to brush their own teeth

questions.question: Which national guidance and programme is this oral care course grounded in?
options: A single hospital's private protocol
NICE guideline NG48, quality standard QS151 and the Mouth Care Matters programme
Only local council policy
The Highway Code

questions.question: Why does a painful mouth put a frail person at particular risk of malnutrition and dehydration?
options: It only affects their speech
It makes chewing and swallowing difficult, so they eat and drink less
It has no effect on appetite at all
It improves how much they eat

questions.question: How does the course describe the importance of a comfortable, fresh mouth for a person's dignity?
options: It only matters at mealtimes
It is pure vanity and not part of care
It protects self-esteem and helps the person stay comfortable with others
It is unrelated to how a person feels about themselves

questions.question: What is 'aspiration' as defined in this course?
options: Food, fluid or saliva entering the airway and lungs instead of the stomach
A type of denture cleaner
Cleaning the teeth twice a day
A hope or ambition a person holds

questions.question: How does keeping the mouth clean help a person who is prone to aspiration?
options: It cures their swallowing problem
It reduces the bacteria in the mouth, lowering the risk of chest infection
It has no effect on chest infection risk
It replaces the need for a speech and language therapist

questions.question: Which of these is a recognised cause of dysphagia mentioned in the course?
options: Using fluoride toothpaste
Brushing teeth twice a day
A stroke, dementia or Parkinson's
Cleaning dentures overnight

questions.question: Whose job is it to assess a person's swallowing if you have concerns?
options: Trained professionals, such as a speech and language therapist
Any care worker on shift
The person's family
No one; concerns should be ignored

questions.question: According to good practice in the course, when should a person's mouth care needs first be assessed and recorded?
options: Only once they develop toothache
Only at their annual review and never sooner
Never, because mouth care is not part of the care plan
When they are admitted to the care setting, written into their care plan and reviewed over time

questions.question: Who carries out the detailed dental assessment of the mouth itself?
options: The registered manager
Dental professionals
The person's next of kin
The care worker giving daily care

questions.question: How often does the course recommend brushing natural teeth?
options: Twice a day, in the morning and last thing at night
Three times every hour
Once a week
Only after meals

questions.question: What does brushing with fluoride toothpaste do for the teeth?
options: Causes tooth decay
Replaces the need to see a dentist ever again
Only whitens the teeth for appearance
Removes plaque and strengthens teeth with fluoride

questions.question: What kind of toothbrush and how much toothpaste does the course recommend for brushing?
options: A denture brush and abrasive cleaner
A small-headed, soft toothbrush and a pea-sized amount of fluoride toothpaste
No brush, just mouthwash
A large stiff brush and a full ribbon of toothpaste

questions.question: Why is brushing before bed especially important?
options: There is no particular reason
Because fluoride only works after dark
Because saliva flow falls during sleep and offers less natural protection
Because teeth only decay at night

questions.question: Why should ordinary toothpaste NOT be used to clean dentures?
options: It dissolves the denture instantly
It is too abrasive and can scratch the denture
It makes dentures taste unpleasant
It is too expensive

questions.question: Why should dentures be brushed over a bowl of water or a soft towel?
options: To make them fit better
To add fluoride to them
So they are not damaged if they slip from your hands
To warm them up before use

questions.question: Leaving dentures out overnight helps to prevent which common mouth infection?
options: A mouth ulcer
Tooth decay in natural teeth
Denture stomatitis, a soreness under the plate
Aspiration pneumonia

questions.question: What does NICE guidance recommend to help identify a person's dentures in a shared setting?
options: Painting them a bright colour
Marking the dentures with the person's name
Never removing them
Keeping them in a communal pot

questions.question: Which of these is a common cause of dry mouth given in the course?
options: Cleaning dentures
Drinking too much water
Medicines, mouth breathing, oxygen or dehydration
Brushing twice a day

questions.question: Which of these is a sign of a dry mouth described in the course?
options: Excess watery saliva
Bright white, glossy teeth
Sticky saliva, cracked lips and a sore or coated tongue
A perfectly comfortable mouth

questions.question: When a person resists mouth care, what is the best response according to the course?
options: Ignore their distress because the plan says to brush
Stay calm, never force it, find out why, offer choice, try again later, and report the difficulty
Decide they no longer need mouth care and never offer it again
Hold their head still and brush quickly before they object

questions.question: Where a person lacks the capacity to consent to mouth care, how must care be given?
options: Following the Mental Capacity Act, in their best interests, gently and without restraint
Not at all, since they cannot agree
By force if necessary to complete the task
Only by the person's family

questions.question: How long should a mouth ulcer or sore patch take to heal before it must be reported and referred for a dental opinion?
options: You should treat it yourself first
A sore lasting about three weeks or more should always be reported and referred
There is never any need to report an ulcer
Only after a full year

questions.question: How might oral thrush (candida) appear in the mouth?
options: As bright, healthy pink gums
As a swollen, painless lump only
As creamy-white patches that leave a red sore area if wiped, or a generally red sore mouth
As perfectly clean, comfortable teeth

questions.question: Who usually provides treatment for oral thrush?
options: The GP or dentist, usually an antifungal
The person themselves without help
No one; thrush always clears on its own
The care worker, using a shop-bought remedy

questions.question: How may a person with dementia who cannot describe pain show that their mouth hurts?
options: By clearly explaining the problem in words
By brushing their own teeth more often
They never show any sign of mouth pain
Through behaviour such as refusing food, pulling at their face or becoming distressed

questions.question: What does the course say about a little bleeding when brushing a neglected mouth?
options: It often settles as cleaning improves, but heavy, persistent or unexplained bleeding should be reported
It should always be ignored completely
It always means the person must go straight to hospital
It means you should stop all mouth care permanently

questions.question: A person has lost weight and their dentures now slip, rub and leave a sore patch. What should you do?
options: Throw the dentures away
File down or adjust the denture yourself
Report the problem and arrange a dental review, keeping the mouth clean and comfortable meanwhile
Tell the person to get used to the soreness

questions.question: What can a dentist do for an ill-fitting denture?
options: Recommend the care worker adjusts it at home
Only advise the person to stop eating
Reline, adjust or replace it to restore comfort and eating
Nothing; a poor fit is permanent

questions.question: Person-centred oral care means what, according to the course?
options: Following a fixed routine for everyone
Cleaning the mouth without agreement to save time
Deciding for the person without asking them
Fitting the care to the individual, beginning with consent and choice

questions.question: Which is an example of respecting a person's cultural or religious needs in mouth care?
options: Supporting practices such as rinsing before prayer, or a carer of the same sex, and following the plan
Refusing to ask about their customs
Insisting everyone uses the same product
Ignoring their wishes to keep to your own routine

questions.question: Which approach is most likely to help when supporting a person with dementia to have their teeth brushed?
options: Give long detailed explanations and insist they keep going when upset
Choose a calm time, use short simple words, demonstrate gently, work in small steps, and stop if they become distressed
Work as fast as possible while they are distracted, without explaining
Skip mouth care altogether because of the dementia

questions.question: When recording mouth care, what should you write?
options: What you actually saw and did, factually and promptly while it is fresh
What you assume happened
Only good news, leaving out refusals
Nothing, as records are optional

questions.question: In the phrase 'you notice and report; the professional assesses and treats', what is the care worker's role?
options: To prescribe antifungal medicine
To adjust dentures and remove loose teeth
To diagnose and treat mouth conditions
To notice problems and report them clearly

questions.question: This oral care e-learning replaces the need for supervised, hands-on training and workplace observation.
options: True
False

questions.question: After brushing with fluoride toothpaste, the person should spit out the excess but not rinse their mouth with water.
options: True
False

questions.question: A dirty mouth carries far more harmful bacteria into the lungs when aspiration happens.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is acceptable to hold a person's head still and force mouth care if they keep closing their mouth.
options: True
False

questions.question: Dentures should be taken out at night to rest the gums and help prevent denture stomatitis.
options: True
False

questions.question: A person with dementia who cannot tell you about pain may show mouth pain through behaviour, such as refusing food or pulling at their face.
options: True
False

questions.question: A care worker should try to adjust or file down an ill-fitting denture themselves rather than involve a dentist.
options: True
False

questions.question: Daily mouth care is part of personal care and belongs in the person's care plan.
options: True
False

questions.question: Why does oral care matter for the people you support? (Select all that apply)
options: It supports eating, taste and nutrition
It removes the need for a dentist
It supports comfort, dignity and social life
Poor oral health is linked to problems beyond the mouth, including chest infections
It qualifies the care worker to diagnose mouth conditions

questions.question: Which of the following are typically recorded in a mouth care plan? (Select all that apply)
options: How much help the person needs with mouth care
Any particular difficulties such as dry mouth or resisting care
The products to use, such as their fluoride toothpaste
The care worker's own dental history
Whether the person has natural teeth, dentures, or both

questions.question: Which are good techniques for brushing a person's teeth? (Select all that apply)
options: Clean along the gum line where plaque gathers
Support the person to brush their own teeth where they can
Scrub hard and fast to finish quickly
Use gentle small circular movements
Cover the outer, inner and biting surfaces of every tooth

questions.question: How should dentures be cared for daily? (Select all that apply)
options: Handle them carefully as they are easily dropped and broken
Clean them with abrasive ordinary toothpaste
Clean them every day and rinse after meals
Store them in a clean, labelled container
Take them out overnight

questions.question: Which of these are reasons to report a mouth problem and arrange a dental opinion? (Select all that apply)
options: Toothache or mouth pain
A clean, comfortable mouth with no problems
Bleeding or swollen gums that do not settle
Loose, broken or lost teeth, or ill-fitting dentures
An ulcer or sore patch lasting around three weeks or more

questions.question: Which are signs of pain in a person who cannot easily tell you in words? (Select all that apply)
options: Rubbing the cheek or jaw
Smiling and eating a full meal with ease
Wincing while eating or avoiding certain foods
Refusing food
Dribbling, disturbed sleep or new restlessness

questions.question: How can you help a person who has a dry mouth? (Select all that apply)
options: Use any saliva substitute gel or spray recorded in the plan
Keep the lips moist with a suitable lip balm
Withhold all fluids until the mouth improves
Encourage regular sips of water and good hydration
Report a persistent dry mouth for review of medicines or a dental opinion

questions.question: Poor oral care can lead to a serious chest infection called ___ pneumonia when material from the mouth is breathed into the lungs.
Difficulty swallowing safely, perhaps after a stroke or with dementia or Parkinson's, is known as ___.
For people with their own teeth, brushing should be done twice a day with ___ toothpaste to strengthen teeth and remove plaque.
Where a person lacks capacity to consent, mouth care must be given in their best interests under the Mental ___ Act.
The advice after brushing is 'spit, don't ___', so a little fluoride is left on the teeth to keep protecting them.
